title: A Voice on the Relay
description: An automated relay, older than the mission, is broadcasting a handshake into the dark. It wants to talk. It may only be an echo.

outcomes.label: Answer the relay
outcomes.description: Trade data with the old machine. Knowledge, and exposure.
outcomes.log: The ship answered the relay and came away with charts and a strange new vocabulary.

outcomes.label: Run silent
outcomes.description: Log its position, tell it nothing, keep the ship's shape a secret.
outcomes.log: The ship passed the relay in silence. Whatever it wanted, it will keep wanting.

title: The Tail
description: A ship with no transponder has matched the voyage's heading for three days. It is not closing. It is not leaving.

outcomes.label: Warn it off
outcomes.description: Light it up and make the ship look expensive to attack.
outcomes.log: A show of teeth sent the shadow drifting back into the dark. Probably.

outcomes.label: Hold course, say nothing
outcomes.description: Give it nothing to react to.
outcomes.log: The ship held course and the tail eventually fell away. The observation decks watched the whole time.

title: The Slowdown
description: The maintenance cohort has stopped working to the letter of a contract the founders wrote for a different ship. Nothing is broken yet. Everything is slower.

outcomes.label: Concede the terms
outcomes.description: Rewrite the cohort's terms in their favor.
outcomes.log: The council conceded the cohort's terms. The work resumed at full speed, and every cohort now knows the council can be moved.

outcomes.label: Hold the line
outcomes.description: The contract stands. Wait them out.
outcomes.log: The council held the founders' terms. The slowdown ended in sullen compliance, and something between the decks did not fully mend.

title: The Convoy Hail
description: A slower ship on a crossing course hails for trade — they have minerals to spare and a hunger for news and seed stock.

outcomes.label: Open the holds
outcomes.description: Deal fairly and part friends.
outcomes.log: Goods and gossip changed hands across the dark. Both ships sailed on the richer for it.

outcomes.label: Wave them off
outcomes.description: Keep to the schedule and the strangers at arm's length.
outcomes.log: The hail went unanswered. The convoy dwindled astern, and the council wondered, later, what news it carried.

title: The Boundary
description: A beacon on the route broadcasts a claim: this stretch of empty dark belongs to a power no one aboard has heard of, and passage requires a toll.

outcomes.label: Pay the toll
outcomes.description: Cheaper than a fight, and faster.
outcomes.log: The toll was paid to a flag no one recognized. The ship passed unmolested, and a little poorer.

outcomes.label: Refuse and press on
outcomes.description: No unseen flag rules the black.
outcomes.log: The ship pressed through the claimed dark, defiant. Nothing came of the threat — this time.

title: The Petitioners
description: A battered craft matches speed and begs to come aboard — a handful of souls fleeing something they will not name.

outcomes.label: Take them aboard
outcomes.description: There is always room for a few more.
outcomes.log: The petitioners came aboard, grateful and guarded. The ship is more crowded, and less alone.

outcomes.label: Turn them away
outcomes.description: The ship cannot carry every stray in the dark.
outcomes.log: The council turned the petitioners away. The craft fell behind, and no one aboard spoke of it at supper.

title: The Tithe Ships
description: A local power intercepts the voyage and names a price for safe passage through their reach of space.

outcomes.label: Pay the tithe
outcomes.description: Credits and cargo are cheaper than a fight.
outcomes.log: The tithe ships took their cut and waved the voyage through. Word travelled that this hull pays.

outcomes.label: Refuse and run
outcomes.description: Burn hard past them and take the hits.
outcomes.log: The ship ran the gauntlet and wore the scars proudly. The tithe ships logged this hull as trouble.

outcomes.label: Offer service, not tribute
outcomes.description: Pay in the one coin the ship mints for free — competence. Offer to survey their reach, mend what they cannot, chart their dark. Skill for passage, and no treasury the poorer for it.
outcomes.log: The voyage bought its passage with a season's work — a survey run, a failing relay rebuilt, a stretch of dark charted for people who could not spare the ships. The local power waved them through with something closer to respect than tolerance, and the crew learned that a ship good at what it does is never truly poor.

title: Boarders on the Hull
description: Grapples ring against the outer plating in the dark between stars. Someone is trying to come aboard uninvited.

outcomes.label: Repel the boarders
outcomes.description: Meet them at the locks. Hold the ship.
outcomes.log: The security decks met the boarders at the airlocks and threw them back into the dark. The ship held, and remembered who held it.

outcomes.label: Open a channel
outcomes.description: Buy passage with cargo instead of blood.
outcomes.log: A price was named and paid through the locks. No one died, and no one felt safe again on that stretch.

title: The Laden Return
description: The holds are heavy with everything the charter was worth, and a heavy ship is a slow, tempting one. Word of the cargo travels ahead on the homeward lane.

outcomes.label: Run dark and quiet
outcomes.description: Kill the beacons, burn fuel to stay ahead of trouble.
outcomes.log: The ship went dark and ran the homeward lane like a smuggler, holds full and running lamps cold. Nothing caught it, and no one slept easy.

outcomes.label: Hire an escort
outcomes.description: Spend part of the prize to guard the rest.
outcomes.log: A hired escort shadowed the ship home for a cut of the cargo. The prize came in smaller and whole, which the council judged a fair trade.

title: The Middle Way
description: A quarrel between decks is curdling toward something worse, and the Meridian Accord — administrators to the marrow — propose a slow, unglamorous arbitration that will satisfy no one entirely and everyone a little.

outcomes.label: Let the Accord arbitrate
outcomes.description: Weeks of hearings, a compromise no one loves, peace that holds.
outcomes.log: The Accord convened its hearings and ground the quarrel down to a compromise as grey as it was durable. No one cheered; no one bled; the ship went on.

outcomes.label: Just rule from the chair
outcomes.description: Skip the process. Decide and move on.
outcomes.log: The captain cut through the Accord's process with a single ruling. It was faster and cleaner and it left a residue of resentment the administrators had meant to dissolve.

title: The Unlearned Watch
description: Generations of peace have hollowed out the security corps — the watch stands its posts by tradition, drills half-remembered, nobody left who has trained for a real breach. A rowdy dispute nearly turns to blood before anyone knows how to stop it.

outcomes.label: Rebuild the corps properly
outcomes.description: Pull the old manuals, run real drills, restore the discipline.
outcomes.log: The watch drilled in earnest for the first time in living memory. The corridors felt safer and a little more guarded, and the corps remembered what it was for.

outcomes.label: Leave the watch ceremonial
outcomes.description: There has been no trouble in a century. Why break the peace to prepare for it?
outcomes.log: The watch kept its polished buttons and its empty drills. The ship felt calm and was not ready, and the knowledge of how to hold a breach slid another notch toward myth.

title: The Open Locks
description: The security systems have decayed to the point of uselessness — locks that won't hold, sensors gone blind, blast doors frozen open. The ship is one bad actor away from a disaster it can no longer contain.

outcomes.label: Refit the security systems
outcomes.description: Spend the parts to make the doors and locks trustworthy again.
outcomes.log: The engineers rebuilt the locks and cleared the frozen doors until the ship could hold itself shut again. It felt, for the first time in a generation, genuinely secure.

outcomes.label: Post human watches instead
outcomes.description: No parts to spare — stand guards where the machines failed.
outcomes.log: With no parts to spare, the ship posted living watches at every failed door. The corridors were held by tired eyes and goodwill, and everyone understood it could not last.

title: The Trade Convoy
description: On the homeward leg the ship falls in with a trade convoy working the lane — they have fuel, parts, and fresh stock, and an appetite for the minerals and salvage filling the holds. The exchange rate is theirs to set.

outcomes.label: Trade cargo for provisions
outcomes.description: Sell minerals and salvage; buy fuel, parts, and food.
outcomes.log: The ship traded a hold of minerals to the convoy for full tanks and stocked lockers. The exchange favored the convoy, as it always does, and the homeward leg felt suddenly survivable.

outcomes.label: Keep the cargo for port
outcomes.description: The minerals are worth more sold at home. Decline.
outcomes.log: The council waved the convoy off and held its cargo for a better price at home. The holds stayed full and the lockers stayed thin, a gamble on making it back before either mattered.

title: The Courts and the Creed
description: The Keepers of the First Flame have grown into the ship's moral authority, and they have run out of patience with the Meridian Accord's endless arbitration — courts that, they say, weigh every truth until it means nothing. They move to dissolve the Accord's standing tribunals.

outcomes.label: Keep the tribunals standing
outcomes.description: Spend the influence to defend the courts against the creed. A ship ruled by faith alone is a ship that stops asking questions.
outcomes.log: The council kept the Accord's tribunals open over the Flame's objection, and the ship stayed a place where a grievance could be weighed rather than judged. The Keepers withdrew, displeased but aboard, and the courts sat on.

outcomes.label: Let the creed rule
outcomes.description: Abolish the tribunals. The Accord will not serve a ship that has replaced its law with a fire.
outcomes.log: The tribunals were struck, and the Meridian Accord — arbiters with nothing left to arbitrate — asked for a hull and left rather than live under a law they could not question. The ship kept its creed and lost the quiet art of settling a quarrel before it drew blood.

title: The Provisioner's Bargain
description: A station hails — an old automated depot, or something crewed and cagey, it is hard to say across the light-seconds — and it has fuel to sell a ship running on fumes. The price is negotiable. That is the problem: the ones who name a negotiable price for what you desperately need rarely mean coin.

outcomes.label: Pay in hard credit
outcomes.description: Empty the treasury for the fuel and owe nothing after. Expensive, clean, done.
outcomes.log: The ship paid the depot's steep price in hard credit and pumped its tanks full and owed the strangers nothing but a wary log entry. The treasury felt the bite for a generation. It was the cheapest thing the ship bought that decade, all told.

outcomes.label: Owe them a favor
outcomes.description: Take the fuel on credit — their kind of credit. Keep the treasury and carry the debt, and whatever carrying it will mean.
outcomes.log: The ship took the fuel on the strangers' terms and sailed away full-tanked and indebted to a name it had not been given, an obligation logged against a day and a price to be named later. The council told itself it had bought time. It had, and borrowed against something worse to do it.

title: The Debt Called In
description: A ship matches course out of the dark and hails on an old frequency, naming a bargain no one now living aboard remembers making — the fuel a desperate generation once took on a stranger's terms. They have come to collect the favor, and they are patient people: they will name the price, and they will wait for the answer, and they do not look like they have ever been told no.

outcomes.label: Honor the founders' word
outcomes.description: The people who made the deal are dust, but the ship that carried them is the same ship. Pay what is asked — cargo, labor, a berth for some of their own — and be free of it clean.
outcomes.log: The ship paid the old favor in full — a hold of ore, a season of its people's hands, and a story that would be told for a while: that this hull keeps its word across generations, that a bargain struck under one captain is still a bargain under their great-grandchildren. The debt closed clean. Some things are worth more than a hold of ore.

outcomes.label: Refuse — the debt died with them
outcomes.description: The ship is stronger now than the beggar that signed. Tell them plainly: the ones who owed you are dead, we owe you nothing, burn off. Keep everything. Carry whatever refusing will come to mean.
outcomes.log: The ship told the strangers their debt had died with the debtors, and burned away before an answer came. Nothing was paid. But a ship that will disown its own founders' word teaches its children a lesson about what a promise is worth, and out in the dark, word travels between the ones who trade in favors.

title: The Marked Hull
description: A waystation the voyage badly needs — fuel, a berth to make repairs, air that someone else has already scrubbed — refuses the ship at the threshold. Word has travelled, the harbormaster says, from ship to ship along the trade-dark: this is the hull that took what it needed and disowned the debt when the bill came. The ones who deal in favors have long memories, and they talk.

outcomes.label: Pay the old debt double
outcomes.description: Buy the name back. Find the ones who were wronged, or their heirs, and settle the founders' broken bargain at twice its worth — the only currency that reopens a closed door is proof the ship has learned to keep its word.
outcomes.log: The ship hunted down the debt it had disowned and paid it double, in coin and in a public accounting, so that the story travelling the dark would change its ending. The doors opened again, slowly. A reputation is cheaper to keep than to buy back, the manifest noted, in effect if not in words.

outcomes.label: Run the closed ports
outcomes.description: The ship owes no one an apology and will not beg for a berth. Make the crossing on its own reserves, past every door that will not open, and be harder for it.
outcomes.log: The ship ran the gauntlet of closed ports on its own reserves, scrubbing its own air and mending its own hull past waystation after waystation that would not have it. It arrived threadbare and proud and entirely self-reliant, having learned the hard way the price of a name — and having decided, deck by deck, to pay it in fuel and hull rather than apology.

title: The Open Hand
description: A settled world hails the passing voyage and offers to fill its holds — food, water, volatiles — and asks for nothing in return. No trade, no favor owed, no price named. Just a gift, freely given by people who remember what the long dark costs. And a ship that has learned to trade for everything, to owe nothing and be owed nothing, discovers it has no ready answer for a gift.

outcomes.label: Accept the open hand
outcomes.description: Take the gift as it is offered, freely, and let the ship be the kind of thing that can receive as well as earn. Full holds, and a debt of the only kind that cannot be collected — gratitude.
outcomes.log: The ship took the gift with both hands and full holds, and something in the founders' proud self-reliance loosened a little — the belief that the voyage owed the universe nothing and could be owed nothing, that every exchange was a ledger. The gardens of that world went with them for a generation in the food and in a quieter change: a ship learning it was allowed to be given to.

outcomes.label: Insist on a fair trade
outcomes.description: The voyage does not take charity. Press payment on them — ore, labor, knowledge — and keep the ledger square and the ship's dignity intact, even if it means leaving with less than an open hand would have filled.
outcomes.log: The ship pressed a fair price on people who had asked for none, and left with square holds and a squarer conscience — the founders' rule kept, that this voyage earns its way and is beholden to no one. The world's people watched the proud ship go and did not entirely understand it, and the ship, keeping its ledger clean, did not entirely understand why it needed to.

title: The Mixed Generation
description: Generations parked in orbit of the host world, and the inevitable has happened: a cohort of the ship's young has grown up half among the hosts — their friends, their loves, their language down on the surface. Some petition to settle and stay. Some are appalled that the question is even asked.

outcomes.label: Grant leave to settle
outcomes.description: Let those who have made a life below stay and live it. The accord deepens into kinship — but the ship sails home lighter, and a little less itself.
outcomes.log: The ship let its mixed generation go to the world that had half-raised them, and the accord became something warmer than any treaty — blood on both sides now. The manifest counted fewer souls for the voyage home, and the founders' descendants understood, not for the first time, that a ship that touches a living world leaves part of itself behind.

outcomes.label: Keep the ship's people whole
outcomes.description: The voyage is not a colony ship and will not be bled one berth at a time. Bring them home, all of them — and let the hosts read the refusal however they will.
outcomes.log: The ship kept its people whole and carried them all home, the way a crew stays a crew. The founding families held; the hosts, watching a door close that they had thought open, cooled a degree toward the visitors who would take their welcome but not their world. Some aboard called it discipline. Some called it a smaller thing.

title: The Hosts' Petition
description: The host people come to the embassy with a need the ship alone can answer — a failing reactor they no longer understand, a plague their medicine cannot name, a library dying for want of the ship's own preserved knowledge. It is not in the charter. It is exactly what an embassy is for.

outcomes.label: Give what they need, freely
outcomes.description: Open the ship's knowledge and stores to the people it came to befriend. Spend from its own reserves and teachers, and let the gift be the accord.
outcomes.log: The ship gave freely — its engineers to their reactor, its physicians to their wards, its archivists to their dying library — and asked for nothing back but the friendship the charter was written to win. The standing it earned was worth more than the charter's pay, though the ship's own teaching benches ran a little thinner for the specialists it lent away.

outcomes.label: Trade the aid on terms
outcomes.description: Help, yes — but the ship is not a charity and its knowledge is its own inheritance. Set a fair price in goods and standing, and keep the ledger and the archive intact.
outcomes.log: The ship helped the hosts on terms — a fair price in ore and standing for its expertise, the archive lent under contract and copied under watch. The need was met and the treasury the richer, and the accord held, cooler and more correct, a friendship that had learned to keep receipts.

title: The Long Residency
description: Generations now among a people not their own, and the ship's children speak the hosts' tongue more easily than the founders'. The embassy has become something the charter never named: a small floating nation that belongs, a little, to two worlds. What is the ship becoming out here?

outcomes.label: Become a bridge people
outcomes.description: Lean into what the residency has made. Let the ship be the seam between two peoples, fluent in both, at home in neither — a new thing, and a useful one.
outcomes.log: The ship chose to be what the long residency had made it — a bridge people, two tongues in every mouth, brokers and translators and kin to a world not their own. The founders' single thread of a culture braided into something wider and stranger, and the voyage that eventually turned home carried a people the launch crew would not entirely have recognized, and would not, perhaps, have entirely approved.

outcomes.label: Stay a guest, hold apart
outcomes.description: Kindness, yes; kinship, no. The ship remains a visitor with a charter and a home to return to, and it will not dissolve itself into a foreign shore, however warm.
outcomes.log: The ship held itself apart — a guest to the end, courteous and whole and quietly homesick — and would not let the residency remake it. The founders' ways stayed intact and a little brittle for the effort, and the crew kept a home in their heads that most of them would never see, and stayed, against every easy current, the ship that had left.

title: The Full Coffers
description: The ledgers have never looked so fat. Contract after contract paid out well, the odd salvage sold high, and the treasury has swollen to a sum the founders would have called a fortune and the living have started, dangerously, to call normal. A rich ship draws opinions on what wealth is for, and they are not all the same opinion.

outcomes.label: Bank it against the lean contracts
outcomes.description: A voyage treasury is not a purse to empty in a good decade. Hold the wealth in reserve against the charter that pays badly, the port that gouges, the repair that cannot wait — a war chest for the years the ledgers will not look so kind.
outcomes.log: The council chose to sit on its fortune rather than spend it, banking the fat treasury into a reserve against the lean contracts everyone knew would come. It won the ship no cheers and no comforts, only the deep, unglamorous safety of a hull that can always cover its own bills.

outcomes.label: Pour it into the ship and its people
outcomes.description: Wealth banked forever is wealth wasted. Spend the surplus on the decks and the folk who crew them — overdue upgrades, open hands, the comforts a hard voyage has long gone without — and let prosperity be felt rather than merely counted.
outcomes.log: The council spent the fat treasury on the ship itself — the habitat decks refit, long-deferred comforts restored, the people's own hands opened — and for a while the voyage felt less like an endurance and more like a life. The coffers came down hard, and the ship that spent them learned, a little, to expect plenty.

title: The Stranger's Trust
description: A passing hauler, met by chance in the deep between waystations, offers the ship generous terms on nothing but its word — no collateral held, no escrow, no lien, only the old spacer's faith that a hull that says it will pay, pays. It is the kind of trust that is worth more than the goods, and it is extended only to those whose name has not yet been spoiled in the trade-dark.

outcomes.label: Accept on trust, and mean it
outcomes.description: Take the generous terms and hold to them absolutely — deliver what was promised, on time, in full — because a name good enough to buy on trust is an asset no treasury can replace, and the surest way to keep it is to deserve it.
outcomes.log: The ship took the stranger's terms on nothing but its word, and made very sure the word was kept — the goods flowing generous and the debt paid to the last credit on the promised day. It cost nothing but honesty, and it bought the one thing a hull cannot mint for itself: the trust of strangers, worth more in the long dark than any hold of ore.

outcomes.label: Insist on formal terms
outcomes.description: Trust is a fine thing and a fragile one; better a smaller deal on paper than a large one on a handshake that a future council might not honor. Decline the generosity, sign a plain contract, and keep the ledger clean of obligations no one alive agreed to.
outcomes.log: The council declined the open hand and signed a plain, smaller contract instead, keeping the ledger clean of trust it might not be able to repay. The hauler shrugged and dealt fair, a little cooler for the refusal — a safe choice, and one that left on the table something the ship had not quite learned how to accept.

title: The Reputation Precedes Us
description: A ship in genuine distress hails on the open band, and it does something unusual before it states its need: it says it knows who you are. Word of this hull travels the trade-dark faster than the hull does, and what the word says is that here is a ship that helps — that took in castaways when it might have taken only their stores, that shared a thin table rather than cull the weakest deck. The stranger is not asking a favor of an unknown quantity. It is asking mercy of a ship famous for it, and offering, in the asking, a trust it would extend to no one else.

outcomes.label: Be the ship they believe you are
outcomes.description: A reputation for mercy is a debt to the version of yourself that others believe in. Answer the distress fully and freely, help without counting the cost, and let the ship's name go on meaning what it has come to mean — because a mercy that holds only when convenient was never mercy, just a habit that had not yet been tested.
outcomes.log: The ship answered the distress as freely as its name had promised, spending its own stores on strangers and asking nothing back, and the trade-dark noted, as it always does, that the reputation had held under test. A name for mercy is a slow thing to build and a fast thing to spend, and the ship, this day, chose to keep meaning what it had come to mean — and found, as merciful ships do, that a name for helping is worth more in the long dark than the stores it costs to keep.

outcomes.label: Trade on the trust instead
outcomes.description: They came because they trust you; that trust is itself an asset, and an asset can be realized. Help, but on terms — extract a hard bargain from a party that came expecting charity, and convert a name for mercy into something more immediately spendable. It works once. Reputations, unlike stores, do not refill.
outcomes.log: The ship helped the strangers, and charged them for it — realizing, in hard credit, the trust its name had earned. It was a shrewd trade and a corrosive one, a party that came expecting charity leaving having bought it, and the story that spread afterward was subtly different from the one that brought them: that this ship, it turned out, would help you, for a price. A reputation spent is not a reputation earned twice.

title: The Feared Name
description: A waystation the ship badly needs to dock at goes quiet as it approaches, and the quiet is not welcome. Word of this hull has traveled the trade-dark too, and the word is colder: that here is a ship that took the stores and left the castaways to the void, that culled its weakest deck to spare the rest, that does the hard thing without flinching and perhaps a little too readily. The station is not refusing the ship — one does not refuse a ship like this lightly — but it deals with it the way one deals with something dangerous: carefully, minimally, and with the exits kept clear.

outcomes.label: Use the fear you've earned
outcomes.description: A name that opens no friendly doors still opens frightened ones. Deal from the reputation you have — let the station's fear do the negotiating, take the docking and the terms a gentler ship would have to ask nicely for, and accept that a hull feared is a hull that gets what it wants, if not what it might once have hoped to be.
outcomes.log: The ship dealt from the fear it had earned, and the frightened station gave it what it wanted on terms a gentler hull would have had to plead for. It was efficient and it was lonely — a people getting its way by being the thing others kept the exits clear of — and the ship banked the ore and the credits and the small cold knowledge that this was who it had become, one hard choice at a time, without ever quite deciding to.

outcomes.label: Begin, slowly, to earn a different name
outcomes.description: A reputation is not a sentence; it is only the sum of what you have done so far, and it can be added to. Deal with the wary station openly and gently, take less than the fear would have handed you, and start — knowing it will take a hundred such choices and years of them — the slow work of being known for something other than the hard thing done readily.
outcomes.log: The ship dealt gently with the wary station and took less than its fearsome name would have handed it, spending the immediate advantage to begin buying, at a steep and slow price, a different reputation. One kind dealing does not unmake a hard name — the station stayed wary to the last — but it was a first entry against a long ledger, the ship choosing, deliberately this time, to start becoming something other than what its choices had made it.

title: The Wary Encounter
description: A hull the ship has never met blocks a passage the ship needs, and there is a long, tense silence on the open band while each vessel decides what the other is. Neither has drawn on the other; neither has stood down. Everything now depends on what happens next, and what happens next depends, more than the council quite expects, on what the stranger has already heard about this ship long before the two of them ever met.

outcomes.label: Withdraw and reroute
outcomes.description: There is no need to make an encounter of it. Peel off, take the longer passage, and spend the fuel to avoid finding out whether the stranger meant trouble — a small cost against an unknown risk, and no shots fired to spread a story either way.
outcomes.log: The ship declined the encounter and took the long way round, spending fuel to keep an unknown quantity at arm's length. No shots, no story, no risk — a cautious captain's choice, and the passage cost only a detour and the small nagging wonder of what the stranger would have done.

outcomes.label: Stand firm and deal hard
outcomes.description: Hold the line and open a hard negotiation from strength — no reputation invoked, no name leaned on, just two wary hulls hammering out terms of passage the old-fashioned way, each watching the other's ports.
outcomes.log: The ship held its line and dealt hard, two wary hulls hammering out a passage on nothing but mutual caution and a close watch on each other's ports. It cost a little to grease and it settled clean, an encounter met on its own terms by a ship that neither hid from it nor leaned on anything but its own nerve.

outcomes.label: Trade on the ship's good name
outcomes.description: The stranger has heard of this ship, and what it heard was good — that here is a hull that deals fairly and helps where it can. Invoke that. Open the band, name the ship plainly, and let a reputation for fair dealing do what no threat could: turn a tense standoff into an easy passage between two vessels, one of which already trusts the other.
outcomes.log: The ship named itself plainly on the open band, and the stranger's whole posture eased — for word of this hull had reached it too, and the word was good. What might have been a standoff became an easy passage and a small exchange of gifts between two vessels, one of which had decided, on reputation alone, to trust the other. A good name, the crew noted, opens doors a gun only ever holds shut.

outcomes.label: Let the ship's feared name do the talking
outcomes.description: The stranger has heard of this ship too, and what it heard was hard — that here is a hull that does not bluff and does not flinch. Use it. Say nothing, do nothing, and let the mere name hang on the open band until the stranger does the sensible thing and clears the passage rather than test a reputation it would rather not learn is earned.
outcomes.log: The ship said nothing, and let its name say everything — and the stranger, having heard exactly what that name meant, cleared the passage and threw in a tribute of ore besides rather than test a reputation it plainly believed. It was the easiest passage the ship ever bought, paid for entirely by the fear it had spent generations earning, and the story that spread afterward made the next such encounter easier still, and the ship a little more the thing that story said.
